title: Crude-Laden Tankers Build in Hormuz as Production Cuts Loom
section_path: Crude-Laden Tankers Build in Hormuz as Production Cuts Loom
section_path_text: Crude-Laden Tankers Build in Hormuz as Production Cuts Loom
summary:
Main
Record crude accumulation and a historic shift in ballast-laden ratios signal deepening supply disruption in the Gulf. Image reference: assets/2026-03-17_crude-laden-tankers-build-in-hormuz-as-production-cuts-loom_img_cea3cf84ceb9ceb3cebcceb9cf8ccf84cf85_df3955f12aab.png
Image reference: unnamed.png | assets/2026-03-17_crude-laden-tankers-build-in-hormuz-as-production-cuts-loom_img_unnamed_5c62f36b5a42.png
Tensions in the Gulf have been escalating for nearly two weeks, culminating most recently in U.S. strikes on military targets on Kharg Island, Iran's primary crude export terminal.

children.title: Main
children.summary:
Record crude accumulation and a historic shift in ballast-laden ratios signal deepening supply disruption in the Gulf. Image reference: assets/2026-03-17_crude-laden-tankers-build-in-hormuz-as-production-cuts-loom_img_cea3cf84ceb9ceb3cebcceb9cf8ccf84cf85_df3955f12aab.png
Image reference: unnamed.png | assets/2026-03-17_crude-laden-tankers-build-in-hormuz-as-production-cuts-loom_img_unnamed_5c62f36b5a42.png
Tensions in the Gulf have been escalating for nearly two weeks, culminating most recently in U.S.

children.title: Laden Tanker Counts Reach Record Levels
section_path: Laden Tanker Counts Reach Record Levels
children.section_path_text: Laden Tanker Counts Reach Record Levels
children.summary:
Recent AIS data show a sharp reversal in the typical ballast-laden balance in the Strait of Hormuz, with laden vessels now significantly outnumbering ballast vessels. Historical ~135 ballast vs ~85 laden vessels
Typical gap 50 vessels in favor of ballast traffic
Recent ~75 ballast vessels vs ~145 laden
Latest crude tanker snapshot 85 laden vs 25 ballast vessels in the latest observations
Image reference: Στιγμιότυπο οθόνης 2026-03-17 132950.png | assets/2026-03-17_crude-laden-tankers-build-in-hormuz-as-production-cuts-loom_img_cea3cf84ceb9ceb3cebcceb9cf8ccf84cf85_91eca8f6c43e.png
During March 2026, owing to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a clear divergence emerged between ballast and laden tanker counts.

children.title: Where the Distortion Is Most Visible
section_path: Where the Distortion Is Most Visible
children.section_path_text: Where the Distortion Is Most Visible
children.summary: The imbalance becomes particularly clear when focusing on crude tankers specifically. Laden crude tanker counts have risen to roughly 85 vessels, while ballast crude tankers have fallen to about 25 vessels.

children.title: Crude Cargo Buildup Inside the Strait of Hormuz
section_path: Crude Cargo Buildup Inside the Strait of Hormuz
children.section_path_text: Crude Cargo Buildup Inside the Strait of Hormuz
children.summary: Based on AIS-derived estimates, approximately 130 million barrels of crude oil are currently onboard tankers in the region, with around 40 million barrels of spare capacity remaining. The pattern is similar for clean product tankers, though on a smaller scale.

children.title: Estimated Daily Transit Capacity
children.summary:
In January 2026, under normal operating conditions, the number of daily tanker transits through the Strait of Hormuz was around 20 each for West to East and East to West (total 40). Image reference: Στιγμιότυπο οθόνης 2026-03-17 133147.png | assets/2026-03-17_crude-laden-tankers-build-in-hormuz-as-production-cuts-loom_img_cea3cf84ceb9ceb3cebcceb9cf8ccf84cf85_2ef2b8ae110f.png
Image reference: Στιγμιότυπο οθόνης 2026-03-17 133154.png | assets/2026-03-17_crude-laden-tankers-build-in-hormuz-as-production-cuts-loom_img_cea3cf84ceb9ceb3cebcceb9cf8ccf84cf85_4239f820bb81.png
Although only one or two confirmed vessel transits per day per vessel class are observed on average, this does not necessarily mean that only that number of ships are passing through the strait.

children.title: Per Commercial Operator | VLCC
children.summary: The operator distribution highlights a notable presence of Asian-linked companies, including Sinokor, Idemitsu, Unipec, COSCO, Bahri, and trading houses such as Trafigura. Their presence among VLCC operators aligns with the high number of tanker transits through the Strait of Hormuz, as these vessels are largely employed in transporting Middle Eastern crude to Asian import markets.

children.title: Tanker Transits and Insurance Environment
children.summary:
Image reference: Στιγμιότυπο οθόνης 2026-03-17 133211.png | assets/2026-03-17_crude-laden-tankers-build-in-hormuz-as-production-cuts-loom_img_cea3cf84ceb9ceb3cebcceb9cf8ccf84cf85_b3d311530f88.png
War-risk premiums for Gulf transits have risen significantly following the recent escalation. Industry indications suggest that premiums have increased from roughly 0.2-0.4% of vessel value previously to around 1% in many cases, with significantly higher levels possible for voyages assessed as carrying elevated risk (from 3% to 5%).

children.title: Volatility Persists as Floating Storage Builds
section_path: Volatility Persists as Floating Storage Builds
children.section_path_text: Volatility Persists as Floating Storage Builds
children.summary: Maritime security across the Gulf remains highly volatile, and tanker operators are likely to remain cautious until clearer signals emerge regarding the stability of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz. In the near term, developments surrounding Iranian export infrastructure, the availability of war-risk insurance, and the willingness of shipowners to deploy vessels in the region will remain key factors shaping tanker freight markets and crude trade flows.
